examples.explanation: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}, add to result [0]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (first unvisited), recursive call to node 1. Step 3: At node 1, mark visited {0,1}, add to result [0,1]. Step 4: Visit neighbor 3 (0 is visited), recursive call to node 3. Step 5: At node 3, mark visited {0,1,3}, add to result [0,1,3], no unvisited neighbors, return to node 1. Step 6: Node 1 has no more unvisited neighbors (0 visited, 3 visited), return to node 0. Step 7: At node 0, visit next neighbor 2, recursive call to node 2. Step 8: At node 2, mark visited {0,1,3,2}, add to result [0,1,3,2]. Step 9: Visit neighbor 4, recursive call to node 4. Step 10: At node 4, mark visited {0,1,3,2,4}, add to result [0,1,3,2,4], no unvisited neighbors, return to node 2, then node 0, completion.

Step 1: Start at node 1, mark visited {1}, add to result [1]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 2, recursive call to node 2. Step 3: At node 2, mark visited {1,2}, add to result [1,2]. Step 4: Visit neighbor 4, recursive call to node 4. Step 5: At node 4, mark visited {1,2,4}, add to result [1,2,4], no neighbors, return to node 2. Step 6: At node 2, visit next neighbor 5, recursive call to node 5. Step 7: At node 5, mark visited {1,2,4,5}, add to result [1,2,4,5], no neighbors, return to node 2. Step 8: Node 2 has no more neighbors, return to node 1. Step 9: At node 1, visit next neighbor 3, recursive call to node 3. Step 10: At node 3, mark visited {1,2,4,5,3}, add to result [1,2,4,5,3], no neighbors, return to node 1, completion.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive DFS
approaches.summary: This approach uses recursion to traverse a graph or tree by exploring as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It is simple to implement for connected structures and naturally follows the DFS definition.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Recursive DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of recursive DFS for graph/tree traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution for implementing recursive DFS traversal. The intuition is that DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, which naturally maps to recursion where each recursive call explores one neighbor before returning. The algorithm starts from a given node, marks it as visited, processes it, then recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. For graphs, we need to handle cycles by tracking visited nodes. The time complexity is O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and visited set. This connects to L1 by using the recursive visit function as the core subroutine. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited before recursion (causing infinite loops in cyclic graphs), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, and stack overflow for deep recursion. Real code pattern: define a recursive function dfs(node) that marks node as visited, processes it, then calls dfs(neighbor) for each unvisited neighbor.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}, add to result [0]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (first unvisited), recursive call to node 1. Step 3: At node 1, mark visited {0,1}, add to result [0,1]. Step 4: Visit neighbor 3 (0 is visited), recursive call to node 3. Step 5: At node 3, mark visited {0,1,3}, add to result [0,1,3], no unvisited neighbors, return to node 1. Step 6: Node 1 has no more unvisited neighbors (0 visited, 3 visited), return to node 0. Step 7: At node 0, visit next neighbor 2, recursive call to node 2. Step 8: At node 2, mark visited {0,1,3,2}, add to result [0,1,3,2]. Step 9: Visit neighbor 4, recursive call to node 4. Step 10: At node 4, mark visited {0,1,3,2,4}, add to result [0,1,3,2,4], no unvisited neighbors, return to node 2, then node 0, completion.

Step 1: Start at node 1, mark visited {1}, add to result [1]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 2, recursive call to node 2. Step 3: At node 2, mark visited {1,2}, add to result [1,2]. Step 4: Visit neighbor 4, recursive call to node 4. Step 5: At node 4, mark visited {1,2,4}, add to result [1,2,4], no neighbors, return to node 2. Step 6: At node 2, visit next neighbor 5, recursive call to node 5. Step 7: At node 5, mark visited {1,2,4,5}, add to result [1,2,4,5], no neighbors, return to node 2. Step 8: Node 2 has no more neighbors, return to node 1. Step 9: At node 1, visit next neighbor 3, recursive call to node 3. Step 10: At node 3, mark visited {1,2,4,5,3}, add to result [1,2,4,5,3], no neighbors, return to node 1, completion.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Visit Function
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that visits a node and its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the recursive function at the heart of DFS. The intuition is that to traverse a graph recursively, we define a function that takes a node, processes it, and recursively calls itself on each unvisited neighbor. This mirrors the DFS definition of exploring depth-first. The algorithm: function dfs(node): if node is visited, return; mark node as visited; process node (e.g., add to result); for each neighbor in adjacency_list[node]: if neighbor not visited: dfs(neighbor). Time complexity for this function on a single call is O(degree(node)), but overall O(V+E) when integrated. Space is O(V) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being the main component called from the wrapper function. Common mistakes: forgetting base case for visited nodes (causes infinite recursion in cycles), not passing adjacency list or visited set correctly, and stack overflow in deep graphs. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, visited, adj_list, result): visited.add(node); result.append(node); for neighbor in adj_list[node]: if neighbor not in visited: dfs(neighbor, visited, adj_list, result).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call dfs(0). visited={}, result=[]. Step 2: Mark 0 visited: visited={0}. Step 3: Process 0: result=[0]. Step 4: Loop neighbors [1,2]. Step 5: neighbor=1, not visited, call dfs(1). Step 6: In dfs(1): mark visited={0,1}, result=[0,1], neighbors [0] (0 visited, skip), return. Step 7: Back to dfs(0), neighbor=2, not visited, call dfs(2). Step 8: In dfs(2): mark visited={0,1,2}, result=[0,1,2], neighbors [0] (visited, skip), return. Step 9: Back to dfs(0), loop ends, return.

Step 1: Call dfs(1). visited={}, result=[]. Step 2: Mark 1 visited: visited={1}. Step 3: Process 1: result=[1]. Step 4: Loop neighbors [2,3]. Step 5: neighbor=2, not visited, call dfs(2). Step 6: In dfs(2): mark visited={1,2}, result=[1,2], neighbors [1,4] (1 visited, skip 4). Step 7: Call dfs(4): mark visited={1,2,4}, result=[1,2,4], neighbors [2] (visited, skip), return to dfs(2). Step 8: dfs(2) loop ends, return to dfs(1). Step 9: neighbor=3, not visited, call dfs(3). Step 10: In dfs(3): mark visited={1,2,4,3}, result=[1,2,4,3], neighbors [1] (visited, skip), return to dfs(1), loop ends, return.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Depth-First Exploration with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Understand the principle of exploring one branch fully before moving to the next.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the fundamental idea behind DFS: explore as deep as possible along a path before backtracking to explore other paths. The intuition is that by prioritizing depth over breadth, we can traverse structures in a way that naturally handles nested or hierarchical relationships. In recursion, this means making a recursive call for one neighbor and only proceeding to the next neighbor after that call returns (i.e., after fully exploring that branch). The algorithm conceptually: start at a node, pick one unvisited neighbor, recursively explore it completely, then return and pick the next neighbor. Time and space are conceptual here, but it leads to O(V+E) and O(V) in implementation. This feeds into L1 by providing the reasoning for the recursive visit function's structure. Common mistakes: confusing with BFS (which uses a queue), not understanding that order depends on neighbor selection, and missing that backtracking happens implicitly via function returns. Real code pattern: the recursive calls in a loop: for neighbor in neighbors: if not visited: dfs(neighbor) ensures depth-first exploration.

examples.input: Start at node A with neighbors [B,C], graph: A-B, A-C, B-D
examples.output: Traversal order: A, B, D, C
examples.trace: Step 1: At A, mark visited, process A. Step 2: Pick first neighbor B (unvisited). Step 3: Recursively explore B: mark B, process B. Step 4: At B, pick neighbor D (unvisited). Step 5: Recursively explore D: mark D, process D, no neighbors, return to B. Step 6: B has no more unvisited neighbors (A visited), return to A. Step 7: At A, pick next neighbor C (unvisited). Step 8: Recursively explore C: mark C, process C, no neighbors, return to A, done.

examples.input: Start at node 1 with neighbors [2,3], tree: 1-2-4, 1-3-5
examples.trace: Step 1: At 1, mark visited, process 1. Step 2: Pick neighbor 2. Step 3: Explore 2: mark 2, process 2. Step 4: At 2, pick neighbor 4. Step 5: Explore 4: mark 4, process 4, no neighbors, return to 2. Step 6: 2 has no more neighbors, return to 1. Step 7: At 1, pick next neighbor 3. Step 8: Explore 3: mark 3, process 3. Step 9: At 3, pick neighbor 5. Step 10: Explore 5: mark 5, process 5, no neighbors, return to 3, then 1, done.

testCases.input: Start at X, neighbors [Y,Z], graph: X-Y, X-Z
testCases.expected: Traversal order: X, Y, Z or X, Z, Y (depends on neighbor order)

testCases.input: Start at A, neighbors [B], graph: A-B-C
Start at 1, neighbors [2,3,4], all disconnected from 1
Start at root, neighbors [left,right], tree: root-left-a, root-right
testCases.expected: Traversal order: root, left, a, right

testCases.input: Start at node P, neighbors [Q,R], graph: P-Q, P-R, Q-S
testCases.expected: Traversal order: P, Q, S, R

testCases.input: Start at alpha, neighbors [beta,gamma], graph: alpha-beta, alpha-gamma-delta
testCases.expected: Traversal order: alpha, beta, gamma, delta

testCases.input: Start at A, neighbors [B,C], graph: A-B-D, A-C-E, B-F
testCases.expected: Traversal order: A, B, D, F, C, E or similar

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Neighbor Iteration
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a graph and iterate through a node's neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing a graph (or tree) in code and accessing the neighbors of a node, which is essential for DFS traversal. The intuition is that DFS requires knowing which nodes are connected to the current node to explore them recursively. Common representations include adjacency list (dictionary or list of lists) where each node maps to a list of its neighbors. The algorithm: given a node, retrieve its neighbor list from the adjacency structure, then iterate through each neighbor. For example, in Python: neighbors = adj_list[node]; for neighbor in neighbors: # process. Time complexity for iterating neighbors is O(degree(node)), and space is O(1) for the iteration. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to pick neighbors for depth-first exploration. Common mistakes: using incorrect data structures (e.g., matrix without optimization), not handling missing nodes, and off-by-one errors in indexing. Real code pattern: adj_list = {0: [1,2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}; for neighbor in adj_list[0]: print(neighbor) outputs 1 then 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access adj_list[2] returns the list [1,4]. Step 2: Iteration: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 4. Step 3: For DFS, we would check if visited and potentially recurse on each.
Step 1: adj_list['A'] gives ['B','C']. Step 2: Loop: neighbor B, then neighbor C. Step 3: In DFS, process B first (if unvisited), then after returning, process C.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Marking Nodes as Visited
ladders.desc: Learn to track visited nodes to avoid reprocessing and infinite loops.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves marking nodes as visited during traversal to prevent revisiting them, which is crucial in graphs with cycles to avoid infinite recursion. The intuition is that in DFS, we want to explore each node exactly once; without tracking, we might loop indefinitely in cyclic structures. The algorithm: maintain a set or boolean array to record visited nodes. When visiting a node, check if it's already in the visited set; if not, add it and proceed. For example: visited = set(); if node not in visited: visited.add(node); # process. Time complexity for checking and adding is O(1) average for a set. Space is O(V) for storing visited nodes. This feeds into L3 by being a prerequisite before iterating neighbors in DFS. Common mistakes: using a list for visited (slow lookup), forgetting to mark before recursion, and not initializing visited properly. Real code pattern: visited = set(); visited.add(0); if 1 not in visited: visited.add(1).

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if 5 in visited: false. Step 2: Add 5 to visited: visited becomes {5}. Step 3: Now 5 is marked as visited.
Step 1: For node 2, check if in visited: true (since 2 is in {1,2}), so skip adding. Step 2: For node 3, check if in visited: false. Step 3: Add 3 to visited: visited becomes {1,2,3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Learn the fundamental concept of recursion: a function calling itself.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: recursion, where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller sub-problems. The intuition is that recursion mimics mathematical induction or hierarchical structures; for DFS, it allows us to explore deeper levels naturally. The algorithm: define a function that has a base case (stopping condition) and a recursive case (calls itself with modified parameters). For example, a recursive function to compute factorial: def fact(n): if n <= 1: return 1; else: return n * fact(n-1). Time and space depend on the problem, but generally, recursion uses stack memory for each call. This feeds into L4 by providing the mechanism for the recursive visits in DFS. Common mistakes: missing base case (causing infinite recursion), not reducing problem size in recursive call, and stack overflow for deep recursion. Real code pattern: def recursive_func(param): if base_case: return something; else: modified_param = change(param); return recursive_func(modified_param).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call fact(3). Step 2: Base case? 3 > 1, so recursive: return 3 * fact(2). Step 3: Call fact(2): base case? 2 > 1, so return 2 * fact(1). Step 4: Call fact(1): base case? 1 <= 1, so return 1. Step 5: Back to fact(2): 2 * 1 = 2, return 2. Step 6: Back to fact(3): 3 * 2 = 6, return 6.

Step 1: Call countdown(2). Step 2: Print 2. Step 3: Base case? 2 > 0, so call countdown(1). Step 4: In countdown(1): print 1. Step 5: Base case? 1 > 0, so call countdown(0). Step 6: In countdown(0): print 0. Step 7: Base case? 0 <= 0, return. Step 8: Return to countdown(1), then countdown(2), done.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative DFS with Stack
approaches.summary: This approach uses an explicit stack data structure to simulate DFS traversal without recursion, pushing and popping nodes to explore depth-first. It avoids recursion overhead and can be easier to manage in languages with limited stack space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Iterative DFS with Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of DFS traversal using an explicit stack without recursion.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution for implementing iterative DFS using a stack. The intuition is to simulate the recursive call stack explicitly using a stack data structure, allowing us to explore nodes depth-first while avoiding recursion overhead. We start from a given node, push it onto the stack, then repeatedly pop a node, process it, and push its unvisited neighbors onto the stack. This mimics the LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) behavior of recursion. The algorithm works because we always explore the most recently discovered node first, which is the essence of DFS. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty stack and a visited set. 2) Push the starting node onto the stack. 3) While stack is not empty: a) Pop a node. b) If not visited, mark as visited and process (e.g., add to result). c) Push all unvisited neighbors onto the stack. Time complexity is O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the stack and visited set. This connects to the full problem by providing the complete working solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark nodes as visited before pushing neighbors (causing infinite loops), pushing visited nodes onto the stack, or using the wrong order for neighbors (for DFS, order matters for traversal path but not correctness). Real code pattern: def iterative_dfs(start, graph): visited = set(); stack = [start]; result = []; while stack: node = stack.pop(); if node not in visited: visited.add(node); result.append(node); for neighbor in reversed(graph[node]): if neighbor not in visited: stack.append(neighbor); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [0], visited = {}, result = []. Step 2: pop 0, not visited, visited = {0}, result = [0], push neighbors 2 then 1 (reversed order), stack = [1, 2]. Step 3: pop 2, not visited, visited = {0,2}, result = [0,2], push neighbor 5, stack = [1,5]. Step 4: pop 5, not visited, visited = {0,2,5}, result = [0,2,5], push neighbors (none), stack = [1]. Step 5: pop 1, not visited, visited = {0,2,5,1}, result = [0,2,5,1], push neighbors 4 then 3 (reversed order, skip 0 as visited), stack = [3,4]. Step 6: pop 4, not visited, visited = {0,2,5,1,4}, result = [0,2,5,1,4], push neighbors (skip 1 as visited), stack = [3]. Step 7: pop 3, not visited, visited = {0,2,5,1,4,3}, result = [0,2,5,1,4,3], push neighbors (skip 1 as visited), stack = []. Done.

Step 1: stack = [1], visited = {}, result = []. Step 2: pop 1, not visited, visited = {1}, result = [1], push neighbors 3 then 2 (reversed order), stack = [2,3]. Step 3: pop 3, not visited, visited = {1,3}, result = [1,3], push neighbors (skip 1 as visited), stack = [2]. Step 4: pop 2, not visited, visited = {1,3,2}, result = [1,3,2], push neighbor 4, stack = [4]. Step 5: pop 4, not visited, visited = {1,3,2,4}, result = [1,3,2,4], push neighbors (skip 2 as visited), stack = []. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Management for Node Exploration
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that manages the stack to explore nodes depth-first.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the main loop that drives the iterative DFS: managing the stack to pop nodes, process them, and push unvisited neighbors. The intuition is that a stack's LIFO property ensures we always explore the most recently added node first, mimicking recursive DFS where the deepest call is processed next. This is key because it replaces the implicit recursion stack with an explicit data structure. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize stack with start node. 2) While stack not empty: a) Pop node. b) If not visited, mark visited and process. c) For each neighbor in reverse order (to match typical DFS order), if not visited, push onto stack. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(V + E) as it processes each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the stack. This feeds into L0 by providing the central engine that the full solution builds around. Common mistakes include pushing neighbors without checking if visited (causing duplicates or infinite loops), or popping without checking visited status (leading to reprocessing). Real code pattern: while stack: node = stack.pop(); if node not in visited: visited.add(node); process(node); for neighbor in reversed(adj_list[node]): if neighbor not in visited: stack.append(neighbor).

examples.input: stack = [A], visited = {}, adj = {A: [B, C], B: [A], C: [A]}, process = append to list
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [A]. Step 2: pop A, not visited, visited = {A}, result = [A], push C then B (reversed neighbors), stack = [B, C]. Step 3: pop C, not visited, visited = {A,C}, result = [A,C], push neighbors (skip A as visited), stack = [B]. Step 4: pop B, not visited, visited = {A,C,B}, result = [A,C,B], push neighbors (skip A as visited), stack = []. Done.

examples.input: stack = [1], visited = {}, adj = {1: [2, 3], 2: [1, 4], 3: [1], 4: [2]}, process = append to list
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: pop 1, not visited, visited = {1}, result = [1], push 3 then 2, stack = [2,3]. Step 3: pop 3, not visited, visited = {1,3}, result = [1,3], push neighbors (skip 1), stack = [2]. Step 4: pop 2, not visited, visited = {1,3,2}, result = [1,3,2], push 4, stack = [4]. Step 5: pop 4, not visited, visited = {1,3,2,4}, result = [1,3,2,4], push neighbors (skip 2), stack = []. Done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — LIFO Stack for Depth-First Exploration
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the use of a stack's LIFO property to achieve depth-first traversal.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight: using a stack's Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) property to explore nodes depth-first. The intuition is that in DFS, we want to go as deep as possible along a branch before backtracking, which matches a stack's behavior where the most recently added item is processed next. By pushing neighbors onto a stack and always popping from the top, we ensure we continue from the last discovered node, simulating recursion. This works because it prioritizes depth over breadth. Step-by-step: 1) Push start node. 2) Pop node (most recent). 3) Push its neighbors. 4) Repeat, always popping the most recent, which leads to depth-first exploration. Time complexity for this logic is O(V + E) as it involves push/pop operations. Space complexity is O(V) for the stack. This connects to L1 by providing the fundamental mechanism that the sub-routine implements. Common mistakes include using a queue (FIFO) instead of stack, which would give BFS, or not reversing neighbor order (affects traversal order but not depth-first property). Real code pattern: stack = [start]; while stack: node = stack.pop(); process(node); for neighbor in neighbors: stack.append(neighbor).

examples.input: start = A, neighbors function: A->[B,C], B->[D], C->[E], D->[], E->[]
examples.output: traversal = A, C, E, B, D
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [A]. Step 2: pop A, process A, push C then B, stack = [B, C]. Step 3: pop C, process C, push E, stack = [B, E]. Step 4: pop E, process E, push nothing, stack = [B]. Step 5: pop B, process B, push D, stack = [D]. Step 6: pop D, process D, stack = []. Done. This shows LIFO: after pushing B and C, C (last in) is popped first, going deep to E.

Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: pop 1, process 1, push 3 then 2, stack = [2,3]. Step 3: pop 3, process 3, push 5, stack = [2,5]. Step 4: pop 5, process 5, stack = [2]. Step 5: pop 2, process 2, push 4, stack = [4]. Step 6: pop 4, process 4, stack = []. Done. LIFO ensures depth-first: after 1, we go to 3 (last pushed) and its child 5 before 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a graph using an adjacency list and access neighbors for traversal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves representing a graph as an adjacency list, which is essential for DFS to access neighbors efficiently. An adjacency list is a dictionary or list where each key is a node, and the value is a list of its adjacent nodes. The intuition is that it provides O(1) access to neighbors, making traversal efficient. For DFS, we need to quickly get all neighbors of a node to push onto the stack. This works because it captures the graph structure compactly. Step-by-step: 1) Define graph as dict: {node: [neighbor1, neighbor2, ...]}. 2) To traverse, for a given node, access graph[node] to get its neighbors. 3) Ensure the graph is undirected or directed as per problem. Time complexity for accessing neighbors is O(1) on average. Space complexity is O(V + E) to store the graph. This connects to L2 by providing the data structure needed for the LIFO stack to operate on. Common mistakes include not handling missing nodes (KeyError), assuming symmetry for undirected graphs, or using inefficient representations like adjacency matrix for sparse graphs. Real code pattern: graph = {0: [1, 2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}; neighbors = graph[0]  # returns [1, 2].

examples.input: graph = {A: [B, C], B: [A, D], C: [A], D: [B]}
examples.output: neighbors of A = [B, C], neighbors of B = [A, D]
examples.trace: Step 1: Represent graph as dict with nodes A, B, C, D. Step 2: For node A, access graph['A'] gives list [B, C]. Step 3: For node B, graph['B'] gives [A, D]. This shows how adjacency list stores connections.
Step 1: Graph has nodes 1,2,3,4. Step 2: graph[1] returns [2, 3]. Step 3: graph[2] returns [1, 4]. This allows quick neighbor access for traversal.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Push and Pop on a Stack
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental stack operations: push (append) and pop.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations of a stack: push (adding an element to the top) and pop (removing and returning the top element). The intuition is that these operations enforce LIFO order, which is crucial for DFS. Push adds new nodes to explore, and pop retrieves the most recent one to continue depth-first. This works because it maintains a sequence where the last added item is always processed next. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty list as stack. 2) Push: use append(item) to add to end. 3) Pop: use pop() to remove and return last element. 4) Check if stack is empty using len(stack) == 0. Time complexity for push and pop is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for n elements in stack. This connects to L3 by providing the operations needed to manage the stack in graph traversal. Common mistakes include using pop(0) (which is queue pop, not stack), or not handling empty stack pops (causing errors). Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(1); stack.append(2); top = stack.pop()  # returns 2; stack is now [1].

examples.input: stack = [], operations: push A, push B, push C, pop, pop
examples.output: stack after ops = [A], popped values = C, B
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push A -> stack = [A]. Step 3: push B -> stack = [A, B]. Step 4: push C -> stack = [A, B, C]. Step 5: pop -> returns C, stack = [A, B]. Step 6: pop -> returns B, stack = [A]. Shows LIFO: last in (C) is first out.

examples.input: stack = [1], operations: push 2, push 3, pop, push 4, pop
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: push 2 -> stack = [1, 2]. Step 3: push 3 -> stack = [1, 2, 3]. Step 4: pop -> returns 3, stack = [1, 2]. Step 5: push 4 -> stack = [1, 2, 4]. Step 6: pop -> returns 4, stack = [1, 2]. Demonstrates push/pop sequence.

testCases.input: stack = [], ops: push A, push B
stack = [A, B, C], ops: pop, pop
stack = [], ops: push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Stack?
ladders.desc: Understand the stack data structure: LIFO order and basic analogy.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what a stack is. A stack is a linear data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, meaning the last element added is the first one removed. The intuition comes from real-life stacks like a pile of plates: you add a plate on top (push) and take the top plate off (pop). This concept is foundational because DFS relies on this LIFO behavior to explore depth-first. It works by maintaining order where recent additions are prioritized. Step-by-step: 1) Start with empty stack. 2) Add items using push. 3) Remove items using pop, always from the top. 4) Peek to see top without removing. Time complexity for basic ops is O(1). Space complexity is O(n). This connects to L4 by introducing the structure that push/pop operations manipulate. Common mistakes include confusing stack with queue (FIFO), or not visualizing the top. Real code pattern: stack = []  # list as stack; stack.append(item)  # push; item = stack.pop()  # pop; if stack: top = stack[-1]  # peek.

examples.input: stack = [], actions: add book1, add book2, add book3, remove one
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push book1 -> stack = [book1]. Step 3: push book2 -> stack = [book1, book2]. Step 4: push book3 -> stack = [book1, book2, book3]. Step 5: pop -> removes book3 (last in), stack = [book1, book2]. Shows LIFO: book3 added last is removed first.

examples.input: stack = [plate], actions: add bowl, add cup, remove one, remove one
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [plate]. Step 2: push bowl -> stack = [plate, bowl]. Step 3: push cup -> stack = [plate, bowl, cup]. Step 4: pop -> removes cup, stack = [plate, bowl]. Step 5: pop -> removes bowl, stack = [plate]. Illustrates LIFO with multiple pops.

testCases.input: stack = [], push hello, push world, pop
stack = [alpha], push beta, pop, push gamma
